summary: Colorado Springs’ verified affordable-housing brief now rests on directly fetched source text: HUD/CSHA voucher status, HomeCOS and Strategic Doing policy documents, city-reported 2022 production and fee-rebate program materials, CHFA tax-credit award announcements, Proposition 123 compliance context, and permanent-supportive-housing / homelessness-response updates.

sections.heading: Colorado Springs Housing Authority and voucher constraints

paragraphs.text: CSHA is HUD-listed as public housing agency code CO028, a combined housing authority in Colorado Springs. CSHA’s official site says the Housing Choice Voucher lottery will not open in July because of current funding limits and that HUD’s pause on issuing new Housing Choice Vouchers remains in effect.

HomeCOS is the city’s affordable and attainable housing blueprint for Mayor John Suthers’ goal to build, preserve, or create purchase opportunities for an average of 1,000 affordable units per year over five years. The plan identifies rental supply, homeownership, and permanent supportive housing for people exiting homelessness as core implementation areas.

Mayor Yemi Mobolade’s 2024 Strategic Doing Framework organized city work around five priorities, including housing solutions. The city’s 2025 Strategic Doing Actions continued those five priorities and reported that 44 of 54 actions set in 2024 were completed, with the remaining 10 rolled into 2025.

The city’s 2022 HomeCOS update reported 1,703 affordable multifamily units under construction or expected to begin construction, 1,236 units anticipated before 2025, and 1,057 affordable units supported during 2022. The same update says the city and Colorado Springs Utilities launched the Affordable and Attainable Multi-Family Fee Rebate Program in the second quarter of 2022.
CHFA’s 2024 Round One Housing Tax Credit announcement listed Sunrise at Shiloh Mesa in Colorado Springs among the developments receiving award reservations, with Greccio Housing named as developer.

CHFA’s 2024 Round Two announcement said 10 developments would receive federal 4 percent and state Housing Tax Credit reservations, including Jet Wing Flats in Colorado Springs, developed by Commonwealth Development Corporation and Solid Rock Community Development Corporation.
CHFA’s 2026 Round One Housing Tax Credit announcement listed Silver Key Apartments at North Weber in Colorado Springs, developed by Silver Key Senior Services, Inc.

sections.heading: Affordable and attainable multifamily fee rebates

paragraphs.text: The city’s fee-rebate program rules apply to multifamily rental projects within Colorado Springs and the Colorado Springs Utilities service area with all units reserved for residents under 120% AMI. Eligible rebates include Land Use Review, PLDO, Public Works, and Colorado Springs Utilities charges, and the scoring rubric gives points for deeper 50% AMI affordability and energy or water-conservation measures.

Proposition 123 participation requires local governments to commit to increasing affordable housing stock by 3% each year in the first three-year cycle and to create a fast-track approval process. A July 2023 Colorado Sun report said only 14 of 336 eligible local governments had applied at that point, while larger municipalities were expected to begin opting in.

sections.heading: Homelessness response and permanent supportive housing

paragraphs.text: Greenway Flats is described by Springs Rescue Mission as Colorado Springs’ first permanent supportive housing project: 65 rooms for chronically homeless residents, operated on a Housing First premise, with residents moved in over the summer before the article was published.

On March 18, 2026, the Pikes Peak Continuum of Care board voted to shift oversight to Pikes Peak United Way and the City of Colorado Springs effective July 1. The Gazette reported that ChangeLine had decided in early 2024 to step away and that about $5.06 million in FY 2025-2026 funding was delayed by litigation over federal formula changes.
The 2026 Point-in-Time Count identified 1,413 people experiencing homelessness, down from 1,745 in 2025, and 308 people experiencing unsheltered homelessness, down from 522 in 2025.
